companyCaseStudy.title: GitHub's gh-ost: Zero‑downtime MySQL Schema Changes on Hundreds of Millions of Rows
companyCaseStudy.summary: Making schema changes at GitHub used to mean minutes-to-hours of locks and replication pain on tables with hundreds of millions of rows. GitHub built gh-ost to perform online schema migrations with near‑zero downtime, moving massive tables (hundreds of millions → billions of rows) with atomic cutovers measured in milliseconds.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

Changing a column on a multi-hundred-million‑row table used to be an all‑hands event: blocking writes, replication lag spiking to minutes, and customer-visible errors during big deploys. GitHub needed a way to evolve schemas safely at scale — without weekend maintenance windows.

## The Problem

- GitHub runs MySQL at massive scale: tables with hundreds of millions (and in some cases billions) of rows.
- Traditional ALTER TABLE (COPY) or in-place DDL under InnoDB could lock the table, causing write stalls or replication lag for tens of seconds to minutes. For big tables this easily became hours.
- Large schema changes meant planning downtime windows, risking live traffic, and complex manual steps.

### Concrete pain points
- Downtime for some ALTER TABLEs: tens of minutes to hours.
- Replication lag spikes: up to hundreds of seconds in high‑write times.
- Operations overhead: manual cutover steps, extensive testing, and emergency rollbacks.

## The Challenge

How do you reliably apply schema changes to massive MySQL tables online, without introducing inconsistency, excessive load, or service disruption — and while preserving semantics (no lost writes)? GitHub needed a repeatable, automated tool that:

- Works with vanilla MySQL replication and binlogs
- Replays live writes to a shadow (ghost) table in near‑real time
- Applies the schema change on the shadow table, then atomically switches traffic
- Throttles to avoid overwhelming masters or replicas

## The Solution — gh-ost (GitHub Online Schema Transmogrifier)

GitHub built gh-ost, an open‑source daemon that performs online schema migrations by streaming MySQL binlogs and applying changes to a shadow table. Key pieces:

- Create a ghost table that has the desired schema change.
- Use the MySQL binlog stream to capture every write (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) on the source table and replay them to the ghost table in near real time.
- Periodically apply throttling based on measured replication lag and server load so the migration doesn't overwhelm the cluster.
- When the ghost table is caught up, perform an atomic RENAME TABLE to swap the ghost with the original — this is a single, very fast metadata operation.
- Optionally drop the old table and resume normal operations.

### Why it works
- The cutover is an atomic RENAME, so application downtime is reduced to the time of a single metadata operation (milliseconds on modern MySQL versions).
- Replaying via binlog preserves write order and guarantees no writes are lost.
- Throttling keeps replication lag and master load under control.

## Architecture (flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  App[Application writes to MySQL master] --> Master[MySQL Master]
  Master --> Binlog[MySQL Binlog]
  Binlog --> ghost[gh-ost process]
  ghost --> GhostTable[Ghost table (new schema)]
  ghost --> Monitor[Metrics & Throttler]
  Monitor --> ghost
  GhostTable --> Cutover[Atomic RENAME TABLE]
  Cutover --> Master
  Master --> App
```

## Example migration flow

1. gh-ost creates `table_ghost` with the new schema.
2. gh-ost starts reading the binlog and replays all writes to `table_ghost`.
3. The throttler monitors replication lag, CPU, and I/O. If lag rises above threshold, gh-ost slows down.
4. When `table_ghost` is fully caught up and application writes have been replayed, gh-ost executes an atomic `RENAME TABLE table TO table_old, table_ghost TO table`.
5. Drop `table_old` when safe.

## Before vs After (real-world style numbers)

| Metric | Before (traditional ALTER) | After (gh-ost) |
|--------|---------------------------:|----------------:|
| Typical table size involved | 100M–1B rows | 100M–1B rows |
| Downtime (application-visible) | minutes → hours (blocking) | <1s (atomic RENAME) |
| Replication lag during change | spike to 100s of seconds | kept < few seconds via throttling |
| Migration wall time | hours → days (full table copy) | hours (replay + cutover), often faster due to online replay |
| Extra disk usage | N/A or temporary copies | +10–30% for ghost table (depends on row size) |

> Note: GitHub reports using gh-ost in production on very large tables (hundreds of millions of rows). The atomic cutover shifts user impact from minutes of blocked writes to a single metadata change.

## Operational details & tradeoffs

- Throttling: gh-ost actively monitors replica lag and will slow itself. This trades migration time for safety.
- Disk overhead: you need space for the ghost table (one copy of the table data while migrating). Plan for +10–30% disk depending on schema.
- Foreign keys: MySQL behaviour with RENAME and foreign keys requires care — gh-ost supports common cases but you must validate constraints.
- Index rebuilds: gh-ost builds the new schema and indexes on the ghost table, offloading expensive index work from the primary table.

## Results & Impact

- GitHub stopped scheduling schema changes only during low traffic windows; changes became routine.
- Downtime for big schema changes dropped from minutes/hours to near zero (atomic RENAME), removing a frequent operational headache.
- Migrations became automatable and safer: gh-ost can be scripted and integrated into CI/CD pipelines.

## Comparison: Options for online schema change

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| In-place ALTER TABLE (native) | Simple, no extra disk | May lock table; long blocking on large tables |
| pt-online-schema-change (Percona) | Popular, streaming with triggers | Uses triggers (can be heavy), more load on master, can cause long-trigger overhead |
| gh-ost | Binlog-driven, low master load, throttling, atomic cutover | Requires binlog access and extra disk; careful with FK edge cases |

## Lessons & Tips

- Ensure binary logging is enabled and you have stable replication — gh-ost depends on it.
- Plan disk headroom before starting a migration; the ghost table is a full copy while building.
- Start with small, noncritical tables to exercise throttles and metrics.
- Automate metrics capture: replication lag, IO wait, CPU, and migration progress. Use those to tune thresholds.

## Why this matters beyond GitHub

Many companies run MySQL at scale and face the same problem: schemas must evolve, but classic ALTERs are risky on big tables. gh-ost demonstrates a pattern: make a shadow copy, stream writes to it, throttle to stay healthy, then atomically swap. This pattern minimizes user impact and makes schema evolution a routine operation instead of a crisis.
keyTakeaways: Use binlog-driven shadow-table replication (gh-ost) to replay writes and achieve near-zero downtime schema changes on very large MySQL tables.
Throttling based on replication lag and system load trades overall migration time for cluster stability — this keeps production healthy while migrating.
Plan for extra disk (ghost table), test FK/index interactions, and automate metrics and cutover to make migrations repeatable and safe.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
gh-ost \
  --user="gh-ost@localhost" \
  --password="${PASSWORD}" \
  --host=master.db.internal \
  --database=prod_db \
  --table=big_events \
  --alter='ADD COLUMN event_type TINYINT UNSIGNED' \
  --assume-master-host=master.db.internal \
  --cut-over=atomic \
  --throttle-control-replicas='replica1,replica2' \
  --hooks='echo "cutover started"' \
  --allow-on-master

concept.title: Bulkheads: Keep One Failure from Sinking the Whole Ship
concept.summary: Bulkhead isolation prevents a slow or failing dependency from consuming all your resources and taking down unrelated requests. It partitions capacity so faults stay local — essential for resilient microservices and distributed systems.
concept.explanation:
## WHY it matters

Imagine a ferry where a hole in one cabin floods the whole ship. In software, a slow downstream service or a hot endpoint can exhaust threads, connections, or CPU and cause your entire service to collapse. The Bulkhead pattern creates compartments so one failure can't sink everything.

**Analogy:** think of a ship with watertight compartments or airplane engines isolated so one engine problem doesn't bring down the whole plane. Bulkheads in software are those watertight doors.

---

## How Bulkheads work (in plain terms)

- Partition resources (threads, connection pools, goroutine pools, containers) per external dependency or per logical feature.
- Limit concurrency/queue depth per partition so overloads are contained.
- Fail fast when a partition is saturated so callers can retry, fall back, or return a meaningful error instead of waiting indefinitely.

### Visual: Flowchart of a request with bulkheads

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client[Client Request] --> API[API Gateway / Service]
  API --> B1{Bulkhead A}
  API --> B2{Bulkhead B}
  API --> B3{Bulkhead C}
  B1 -->|Limited threads| SvcA[Service A]
  B2 -->|Limited threads| SvcB[Service B]
  B3 -->|Limited threads| SvcC[Service C]
  SvcA --> API
  SvcB --> API
  SvcC --> API
```

### Visual: Sequence (what happens when downstream slow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>API: Request X
  API->>BulkheadA: Try acquire slot
  BulkheadA-->>API: Slot acquired
  API->>SvcA: Call
  SvcA-->>API: Slow response
  API->>BulkheadA: Release slot
  API-->>Client: Response

  Client->>API: Request Y
  API->>BulkheadA: Try acquire slot
  BulkheadA-->>API: No slots (fail fast)
  API-->>Client: 503 or fallback
```

---

## BEFORE / AFTER (concrete comparison)

| Situation | Before (no bulkhead) | After (with bulkheads) |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| A downstream spikes | Exhausts threadpool, queue grows, high latency across *all* endpoints | Only the partition for that dependency hits limit; other features remain responsive |
| Error visibility | Hard to correlate; whole service degrades | Failures localized; easier to detect and handle per dependency |
| Retry behavior | Retries amplify load globally | Retries can be limited to failing partition; use backoff/fallback |
| User impact | Large blast radius | Small blast radius

---

## When to use bulkheads

- You have multiple downstream dependencies (databases, third-party APIs, internal services).
- A single misbehaving dependency can consume shared resources (thread pools, DB connections, sockets).
- You want predictable latency and graceful degradation rather than total outage.

Not necessary for trivially small systems where a single pool is simpler and resources are abundant.

---

## Types of bulkheads

- **Thread-pool / worker-pool bulkhead**: assign a dedicated thread or worker pool per dependency (common in Java with Hystrix-style thread isolation).
- **Semaphore bulkhead**: limit concurrent calls with a semaphore (less resource heavy than full thread pools).
- **Connection-pool bulkhead**: separate connection pools per downstream system.
- **Process / container bulkhead**: run services in separate processes/containers/nodes so OS-level limits isolate failures.
- **Network-level**: rate limit or shape traffic per route to prevent overload.

---

## Code examples

### Java + Resilience4j (Semaphore Bulkhead via annotation)

```java
// application.yml
resilience4j.bulkhead.instances.backendA.maxConcurrentCalls: 10
resilience4j.bulkhead.instances.backendA.maxWaitDuration: 0

// Service code
@Bulkhead(name = "backendA", type = Bulkhead.Type.SEMAPHORE)
public String callBackendA() {
  return restTemplate.getForObject("https://api.backend/a", String.class);
}
```

Explanation: This limits concurrent calls to 10. When saturated, callers immediately get a BulkheadFullException which you can handle with a fallback.

### Node.js small semaphore (async/await)

```js
// Simple semaphore for per-dependency concurrency
class Semaphore {
  constructor(limit) { this.limit = limit; this.queue = []; this.count = 0 }
  async acquire() {
    if (this.count < this.limit) { this.count++; return }
    await new Promise(res => this.queue.push(res));
    this.count++;
  }
  release() {
    this.count--;
    if (this.queue.length) this.queue.shift()();
  }
}

const backendAsem = new Semaphore(5);

async function callBackendA(req) {
  await backendAsem.acquire();
  try {
    return await fetch('https://api.backend/a');
  } finally { backendAsem.release(); }
}
```

(Also consider battle-tested libraries: Resilience4j, Polly for .NET, Bucket4j, or token-bucket/queue libraries for Node.)

---

## Common pitfalls & how to avoid them

- **Wrong sizing**: too small -> unnecessary errors; too large -> ineffective isolation. Start conservative and adjust with metrics and load testing.
- **Resource fragmentation**: many tiny pools may waste memory or threads. Use semaphores where possible instead of full thread pools.
- **Blind replication**: applying identical limits to all services without considering real capacity is misleading.
- **Retries + bulkheads = storms**: if callers retry aggressively on bulkhead rejections, you can create synchronized spikes. Use jittered exponential backoff and global retry budgets.
- **Missing observability**: without per-bulkhead metrics (reject rate, queue length, concurrency) you won't know when to tune them.
- **Combining with timeouts**: set sensible timeouts so workers are not held indefinitely; pair timeouts with bulkheads for predictable behavior.

---

## Best practices

- Monitor: concurrent usage, rejection rate, latency per bulkhead.
- Start with semaphores for lightweight isolation; use dedicated pools when blocking operations would otherwise consume shared threads.
- Pair with circuit breakers, rate limiting, and backpressure to form a resilient strategy.
- Run chaos experiments that simulate a downstream failure to validate your bulkhead sizing and fallbacks.
- Provide meaningful fallbacks: cached responses, degraded functionality, or clear error messages.

---

## TL;DR

- Bulkheads partition resources so a failure or overload in one area doesn't cascade.
- Use semaphores, separate pools, or separate processes depending on resource type and blocking behavior.
- Watch metrics, size cautiously, and pair with retries/circuit breakers and fallbacks.
keyTakeaways: Bulkheads isolate failures by partitioning capacity (threads, connections, containers) per dependency or feature.
Prefer semaphores for lightweight isolation; use separate pools/processes when blocking work would starve shared resources.
Monitor rejection rates and pair bulkheads with circuit breakers, timeouts, and smart retries to avoid amplification.
